reading.intro: How to turn a brittle fifteen page setup document into a run you can trust.

blocks.text: From a fifteen page setup doc to one reproducible run

Right now the eval setup is a fifteen page document full of manual steps. Someone opens it, follows the clicks, copies the values, and hopes nothing drifted since last time. That is tribal knowledge. It lives in one person's head and in a stale doc, it breaks when a default changes, and no two people run it the same way. Frank's highest leverage contribution as an infra engineer is to delete that doc and replace it with reproducible one shot runs that anyone can trigger and that produce the same result every time.

blocks.definition: A tool where you write a config that describes the cloud resources you want, and Terraform figures out how to create them. You declare the desired end state, you do not script each manual step, so the same config produces the same resources on every run.

Terraform plan shows you a readable diff of exactly what will be created, changed, or destroyed before anything happens. Apply then performs that diff. You review the change first, so nothing surprising touches the cloud.

A Docker image that pins the exact code and a specific Gemmaclaw benchmark commit. Because the inputs are locked to one commit, the run is deterministic, and a result can be traced back to the exact code that produced it.

A Cloud Run job that starts, runs exactly one eval against one model build, writes its results, and then exits. It is not a long lived server. It runs once and stops, which is what makes a single clean run possible.

Teardown that is safe to run twice. Applying an empty desired set from any state leaves nothing behind, and running teardown again changes nothing, so resources never linger and never keep costing money.

blocks.text: The lifecycle is five clean stages. Terraform provisions the cloud resources from the config. A Docker image pins the exact code and the benchmark commit so the inputs are deterministic. Cloud Run executes exactly one job against one model build. Results are emitted to durable storage so they outlive the run. Then everything tears down so nothing lingers or keeps billing. The whole thing is codified, so the next person just triggers it.

items: Identical reproducible environments every run, so there are no snowflake machines that only one person can rebuild.
A diff you can read and review before you apply it, because plan shows exactly what will change ahead of time.
Automatic rollback when an apply fails, so a broken change does not leave the system half configured.

blocks.title: The manual doc against the codified run
blocks.body: The manual path is fifteen pages of clicks that drift, and a failed step halfway through leaves a half built environment nobody wants to clean up. The codified path is one command. Terraform plans the diff, you read it, you apply, Cloud Run runs the single job, results land in durable storage, and teardown removes every resource. Same result today, next week, and on a teammate's machine.

blocks.text: The goal is not to automate the fifteen pages faster. It is to make the run so reproducible that the fifteen page doc stops existing.

reading.summary: A fifteen page manual setup doc is tribal knowledge that breaks and cannot be reproduced. Codifying it into one shot runs gives identical environments every time, a reviewable plan diff before any change, and automatic rollback on failure. Terraform provisions, a pinned Docker image makes inputs deterministic, Cloud Run runs one job, results are emitted to durable storage, and idempotent teardown leaves nothing behind.

diagrams.title: The one shot run lifecycle
diagrams.mermaid:
flowchart LR
  T[Terraform provisions resources] --> D[Docker image pins code and commit]
  D --> R[Cloud Run runs one job]
  R --> E[Emit results to durable storage]
  E --> X[Teardown leaves nothing]
diagrams.takeaway: Provision, pin, run once, emit, tear down. Every stage is codified, so the run is reproducible and nothing lingers.

audio.script:
Leo: Today the eval setup is a fifteen page document full of manual steps. That is tribal knowledge, and tribal knowledge breaks.

Maya: So how do we make a run we can actually trust?

Leo: We codify it as one shot runs. Terraform provisions the cloud resources from a config that describes what we want. A Docker image pins the exact code and a specific Gemmaclaw benchmark commit, so the inputs are deterministic. Then Cloud Run executes exactly one job against one model build.

Maya: And after the job finishes?

Leo: Results are emitted to durable storage, then everything tears down so nothing lingers or costs money. The wins are real. Every run gets an identical environment, we read a plan diff before we apply it, and a failed apply rolls back automatically instead of leaving a half configured mess.

scene.title: One reproducible run, start to teardown
scene.description: The lifecycle of one reproducible eval run, from Terraform provisioning through a pinned Docker image and a single Cloud Run job to emitted results and a clean teardown, contrasted with the brittle manual doc.

panels.title: The one shot lifecycle
panels.description: The five codified stages a reproducible run moves through, each one automated so the whole run is deterministic.

panels.title: Why infrastructure as code wins
panels.description: The three benefits that make a codified run beat the fifteen page manual doc, shown against the manual baseline.

cues.label: The brittle doc
cues.headline: Fifteen pages of manual steps
cues.narration: Today the eval setup is a fifteen page manual document, and that tribal knowledge breaks.
cues.pass: the need for a codified run

cues.label: Provision and pin
cues.headline: Terraform provisions, Docker pins
cues.narration: Terraform provisions the resources from a config, and a Docker image pins the code and the benchmark commit.

cues.label: Run one job
cues.headline: Cloud Run runs exactly once

cues.label: The three wins
cues.headline: Identical, reviewable, reversible
cues.narration: Every run gets an identical environment, a plan diff you read before apply, and automatic rollback on failure.

interactive.title: Trace a one shot run
interactive.instructions: Step through the lifecycle. Provision with Terraform, pin the Docker image to a benchmark commit, run one Cloud Run job, emit the results, then tear down. Toggle a failing apply to watch the automatic rollback leave the system untouched, and read the plan diff before each apply.

code.prompt: Model an idempotent one shot run lifecycle. Given a desired resource set and the current state, compute a plan (what to create, what already exists, what to destroy), then apply it and return the resulting state. Teardown is just an empty desired set, and it must be idempotent, so running it from any state, and running it twice, always leaves an empty state. Fill the TODOs, then compose them.
code.starter_code:
def compute_plan(desired, current):
    # desired and current are lists of resource names.
    # TODO: return a dict with three sorted lists:
    #   'create'  = in desired but not current
    #   'keep'    = in both desired and current
    #   'destroy' = in current but not desired
    pass


def apply_plan(current, plan):
    # TODO: start from the current set, add everything in plan['create'],
    # remove everything in plan['destroy'], and return the state as a sorted list.
    pass


def run_once(desired, current):
    plan = compute_plan(desired, current)
    return {"plan": plan, "state": apply_plan(current, plan)}
constraints: Use plain Python sets and dicts, no cloud libraries.
compute_plan must return sorted lists so the output is deterministic.
Teardown is an empty desired set. run_once([], anything) must return an empty state, and running it twice must stay empty.

walkthrough.title: From desired and current to the next state

steps.title: Diff the two sets
steps.detail: compute_plan turns desired and current into sets and takes the differences. Create is desired minus current, destroy is current minus desired, keep is the intersection.

steps.title: Apply the diff
steps.detail: apply_plan starts from current, adds the create list, removes the destroy list, and returns the sorted result, which always equals the desired set.

steps.title: Teardown is idempotent
steps.detail: Passing an empty desired set makes create empty and destroy the whole current set, so the state becomes empty. Running it again keeps it empty, which is what idempotent means.

io_examples.label: provision from scratch
io_examples.explanation: Nothing exists yet, so both resources are created and the state holds both, sorted.

io_examples.label: teardown to nothing
io_examples.explanation: An empty desired set destroys everything, leaving an empty state. Running it again stays empty, so teardown is idempotent.

visualization.title: desired plus current to plan to state
visualization.description: How the diff between desired and current becomes a plan, and how applying that plan yields the next state.

worked_examples.label: basic
worked_examples.title: Explicit set differences
worked_examples.explanation: compute_plan diffs the two sets into create, keep, and destroy. apply_plan adds the creates and removes the destroys, and the sorted result always equals the desired set.

worked_examples.label: concise
worked_examples.title: State equals the desired set
worked_examples.explanation: Applying create then destroy leaves exactly the desired set, so an empty desired always yields an empty state, which makes teardown idempotent.

hints.text: Turn desired and current into sets so you can take differences.
create is desired minus current, destroy is current minus desired, keep is the intersection.
Wrap each result in sorted(...) so the lists are deterministic.
In apply_plan start from set(current), add the create list, then remove the destroy list.
An empty desired set makes create empty and destroy everything, so run_once([], current) returns an empty state, and running it again stays empty.

tests.description: provisioning from an empty state creates everything
a mixed desired and current yields create, keep, and destroy

hidden_tests.description: teardown from an already empty state stays empty

questions.prompt: Today, how is the eval setup documented?
choices: A fifteen page manual setup document full of steps
A single Terraform config in version control
A Cloud Run one shot job
questions.explanation: The current setup is a brittle fifteen page manual document, which is tribal knowledge that breaks and cannot be reproduced.

questions.prompt: Which statements about the one shot lifecycle are true? Select all that apply.
choices: Terraform provisions the cloud resources from a declarative config
The manual doc makes each run deterministic
A Docker image pins the code and a specific benchmark commit
Cloud Run keeps the job running forever after it finishes
questions.explanation: Terraform provisions declaratively and the Docker image pins the commit. The manual doc is not deterministic, and a Cloud Run one shot job exits after it runs once.

questions.prompt: Which of these does the codified one shot run rely on? Select all that apply.
choices: A fifteen page manual setup document
Engineers memorizing the steps as tribal knowledge
Leaving resources running after the job to save setup time
questions.explanation: None of these. The whole point is to replace the manual doc and tribal knowledge, and teardown removes resources rather than leaving them running.

questions.prompt: Which of these are genuine properties of the codified approach? Select all that apply.
choices: A pinned commit makes the inputs deterministic
Idempotent teardown can run twice and still leave nothing
questions.explanation: All three are core properties. Declarative provisioning, a pinned commit for determinism, and idempotent teardown together make the run reproducible.

questions.prompt: Put the one shot run lifecycle stages in order.
items: Cloud Run executes one job against one model build
Docker pins the code and the benchmark commit
Results are emitted to durable storage
correct_order: Docker pins the code and the benchmark commit
Cloud Run executes one job against one model build
Results are emitted to durable storage

questions.prompt: Explain why reproducible one shot runs beat the fifteen page manual setup document, and name the three infrastructure as code benefits.
questions.actual_answer: The fifteen page manual document is tribal knowledge. It lives in one person's head and a stale doc, it drifts when defaults change, and no two people run it the same way, so results cannot be reproduced or trusted. A codified one shot run fixes this. Terraform provisions the resources from a declarative config, a Docker image pins the exact code and benchmark commit so the inputs are deterministic, Cloud Run executes exactly one job, results are emitted to durable storage, and idempotent teardown removes everything so nothing lingers or costs money. The three infrastructure as code benefits are identical reproducible environments every run with no snowflake machines, a plan diff you can read and review before you apply it, and automatic rollback when an apply fails so a broken change never leaves the system half configured.
questions.rubric: Full credit: explains that the manual doc is unreproducible tribal knowledge that drifts, and names all three benefits, identical reproducible environments, a reviewable plan diff before apply, and automatic rollback on failure. Partial credit: names some benefits or only contrasts the doc without the three benefits. Low credit: vague or missing the reproducibility point.
